headline: Northern Pacific Golden Spike Driven at Gold Creek, 1883
body:
Well now, gather 'round, and let old Twain spin ye a yarn about a day out in Montana Territory, back in '83, when they aimed to tie the ends of this great Northern Pacific iron snake together. September the eighth, it was, a day they’d been hankerin’ for since the first shovel of dirt was turned. The place was Gold Creek, a spot where a fellow might once have found a shiny nugget or two, though on this particular day, the real treasure was the clatter and hiss of a locomotive. 

They called it the “golden spike” ceremony, a grand affair, though if you’d been there, you’d have seen it wasn’t gold at all, but plain old iron, the very same spike, they say, that started the whole shebang back in Minnesota a dozen years prior. A bit of a humbug, perhaps, but then, what grand human endeavor ain't got a touch of showmanship to it? Henry Villard, the big boss of the Northern Pacific, he was there, puffed up with pride, and former President Ulysses S. Grant, a man who knew a thing or two about bringing things to a conclusion, was on hand too. They even had a head chief of the Crows, and a little tyke named Hilgard, whose tiny hands were made to touch the spike, a symbol, I reckon, of the future they were all hammering into place. 

Three hundred guests, they came, from the East, from across the great waters even, all crammed into four trains, with another train chugging in from the Pacific side. They’d left a thousand feet of track unfinished, just for the spectacle of it, to show these city slickers how fast a crew could lay down steel. And when that last rail was in place, and the moment came for the final blow, Villard himself, with Grant and Henry C. Davis by his side, took up the hammer. 

Old Villard, he wrote in his memoirs, “What wonder that he felt indescribably elated at this consummation of his peaceful conquest of the West?” A peaceful conquest, he called it, though the iron horse often brought more than just peace to the lands it traversed. The roar of artillery, the military music, and the wild cheering that day, they weren't just for the railroad, but for the notion of a continent tamed, a wilderness brought to heel by the relentless march of progress. The Northern Pacific, it wasn't just a line of track; it was a promise, a gamble, and a mighty big one at that, connecting the Great Lakes to the Pacific Coast, and forever changing the face of Montana. The trains would now carry goods, and folks, and dreams, across vast stretches of land, opening up new territories for settlement and commerce, binding this wild western country tighter to the rest of the nation. It was a grand day, indeed, full of sound and fury, signifying, if not gold, then certainly a future as wide and open as the Montana sky itself.
